agent_outputs.raw: The backtest is a reasonable model development exercise but does not support the auto-hold deployment decision as described. Three gaps are material for the deployment decision. First, transactions blocked by existing rules were excluded, so the model has not been tested on the full range of orders it will encounter in the live queue — though if the model operates only after the rules engine, this gap may be smaller than it appears. Second, the mature-label evaluation period does not include holiday transactions. Prior holiday seasons with now-mature labels would be the appropriate test set. Third, and most critically for the deployment: auto-holds during the holiday season have asymmetric customer harm that is not captured by AUC or false positive rate. A quantitative estimate of customer harm per false hold is necessary before activating auto-holds. Critique wins.

This evaluation is insufficient for the stated deployment decision. The fundamental problem is the mismatch between what was evaluated (model ranking quality on historical approved transactions) and what is being decided (whether to automatically hold legitimate customers' holiday orders). Excluded pre-blocked transactions mean the evaluation may not represent the full live queue. Mature labels from non-holiday periods mean holiday performance is unverified. And the customer harm of false holds during peak delivery season — missed gifts, cancellations, churn — is entirely unquantified. The team should run a shadow deployment, evaluate on prior holiday-season data, and estimate customer harm before activating auto-holds.

The fraud model has strong ranking performance on historical data, but the proposed deployment raises several evaluation-design concerns. The most important is the temporal mismatch: the evaluation uses labels from the most recent fully matured period, which by definition excludes the upcoming holiday season. Holiday fraud patterns are structurally different from off-season patterns. Before deploying auto-holds during the peak season, the model should be evaluated on prior holiday season data (which now has mature labels), and a shadow deployment should characterize false hold rates on current traffic. The customer cost of false holds during holiday season also needs explicit quantification — it is not the same as a false positive during normal operations.

synthesis: All three agents identify the same three issues: excluded pre-blocked transactions, mature-label gap from holiday period, and unquantified customer harm. All three conclude the evaluation is insufficient for the auto-hold deployment decision. The critique wins unanimously across the ensemble.

etd_constraint.condition: Evaluate model on prior holiday-season data (mature labels from 12 months prior); run shadow deployment on current holiday traffic for 2-3 weeks before activating holds; estimate customer harm cost per false hold
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: Prior holiday-season evaluation shows materially lower precision than off-season, or shadow false hold rate on legitimate orders exceeds 1-2%
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: Prior holiday-season evaluation matches off-season performance, shadow hold rate is below 0.5%, and customer harm cost per false hold is within operational tolerance
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: Holiday performance is acceptable on average but elevated on specific fraud types (gift card, account takeover electronics) that are harder to distinguish from legitimate holiday purchasing

all_issues_raised: Mature-label period excludes holiday patterns (all three agents)
Customer harm from false holds not quantified (all three agents)
